network.overall_strategy: This network follows the literature by treating racemic alanine as the experimentally supported convergent product of multiple prebiotic routes, then connecting that racemate to L-alanine only through a clearly labeled biochemical/prebiotic symmetry-breaking module grounded in terrestrial homochirality hypotheses rather than direct abiotic alanine-selective synthesis. The network therefore combines: (i) hydrothermal generation of key carbonyl and keto-acid hubs such as pyruvate and acetaldehyde; (ii) surface Strecker, cyanosulfidic, HCN-oligomer, and spark-discharge routes to DL-alanine or its immediate precursors; and (iii) a final biochemical selection stage in which an RNA-mediated homochiral environment biases incorporation/retention of L-alanine, reflecting the literature conclusion that direct prebiotic synthesis yields racemic alanine and that homochirality likely emerged as a network-level terrestrial property rather than a single alanine-forming reaction.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formaldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, pH 9-11, serpentinization-derived H2

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on iron-sulfur phases gives reduced C1 products including formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde generation from H2 and CO2 on Fe(Ni)S catalysts under alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions is supported by vent-origin models and experimental validation (Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal setting; compatible with iron-promoted reverse TCA-like chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise mineral-mediated CO2 reduction and C-C bond formation produce pyruvate as a central alpha-keto acid.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate formation from CO2 under prebiotic iron-rich conditions is supported by CO2 reduction over Fe/Ni nanoparticles and iron-promoted reverse TCA analog chemistry (Beyazay et al., 2023; Muchowska, Varma, and Moran, 2019).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type aldehyde generation to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type regime producing aldehydes and related reduced organics

reactions.mechanism: CO hydrogenation on reduced iron mineral surfaces generates reduced carbonyl compounds including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on Fe/Ni mineral surfaces is reported to generate organics including amino-acid-relevant carbonyl precursors, and alanine itself was detected in related hydrothermal product mixtures (Huber and Wächtershäuser, 2006). The use here as an acetaldehyde source is a literature-grounded network connection rather than a claim of high selectivity.

reactions.name: Surface photochemical generation of acetaldehyde from formaldehyde-rich carbonyl pool

conditions.other: UV-irradiated shallow pool; aldehyde stabilization/concentration by evaporitic mineral assemblages

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven photoredox homologation of C1 carbonyl chemistry furnishes C2 aldehydes including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Acetaldehyde is identified in the literature review as forming prebiotically by UV photolysis of formaldehyde solutions and related photoredox carbonyl chemistry; aldehyde precursor generation under UV photochemistry is supported by Ritson and Sutherland (2013). TiO2 is included to satisfy required surface mineral specification as a plausible photocatalytic mineral in UV-exposed settings.

reactions.name: Surface spark-discharge production of HCN and acetaldehyde from reduced gases

conditions.temperature: ambient to warm, 300-370K aqueous collection phase
conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge with mineral collection surfaces
conditions.other: classic Miller-Urey atmospheric discharge over one week; products dissolve into aqueous phase

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge through reduced atmospheric gases generates nitriles and aldehydes in situ, feeding subsequent Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Miller's classic discharge experiment produced amino acids through in situ generation of HCN and carbonyl compounds; alanine was the second most abundant amino acid and Strecker chemistry was later identified as the operative mechanism (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008). Surface minerals here represent plausible collection/concentration media rather than the discharge source itself.

reactions.name: Surface volcanic spark-discharge with H2S enhancing HCN and acetaldehyde precursor pool

conditions.temperature: ambient to warm, 300-370K aqueous collection phase
conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge with sulfur-rich volcanic gases
conditions.other: volcanic Miller variant; H2S-containing atmosphere increases amino acid productivity

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge in sulfur-bearing reduced gases enhances formation of nitriles and aldehydes that subsequently enter Strecker synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: The H2S-containing Miller apparatus produced alanine yields more than an order of magnitude higher than the classic setup and gave amino acid distributions resembling Murchison (Parker et al., 2011). Surface sulfide minerals are included because the environment is sulfur-rich and the specification requires mineral agents.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic generation of HCN feedstock and aldehyde precursor pool

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling in UV-irradiated phosphate-rich solution
conditions.other: neutral pH; systems chemistry setting producing amino-acid, lipid, and nucleotide precursors simultaneously

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven cyanosulfidic photoredox chemistry reductively homologates cyanide-derived carbon to aldehydic precursors including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. established HCN/H2S/UV/phosphate systems chemistry at 35°C, and Ritson and Sutherland demonstrated photoredox routes to aldehyde precursors needed for aminonitrile formation (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker addition from acetaldehyde to alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions, acidic or basic pH; concentrated NH3 and HCN required

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, followed by cyanide addition to yield alpha-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the canonical Strecker route for alanine, first demonstrated by Strecker and identified by Miller as the operative mechanism in discharge experiments; nitrogen isotope partitioning in alpha-aminopropionitrile also supports this pathway in meteoritic organics (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker synthesis of alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: UV-driven Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox network in neutral solution
conditions.other: neutral pH; reported approx. 70% yield of alpha-aminopropionitrile from acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Phosphoro-Strecker chemistry under cyanosulfidic photoredox conditions converts acetaldehyde efficiently into alpha-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. reported alpha-aminopropionitrile formation at about 70% yield from acetaldehyde in cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker chemistry at 35°C, directly supporting this step (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile to alaninamide

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis following Strecker aminonitrile formation

reactions.mechanism: Partial nitrile hydrolysis converts the aminonitrile to the corresponding amide.
reactions.reasoning: Sequential hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile through alaninamide to alanine is the accepted Strecker completion sequence for alanine (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alaninamide to DL-alanine

conditions.other: completes Strecker sequence to racemic alanine

reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis furnishes free alanine; the product is racemic under abiotic conditions.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker route to alanine proceeds through aminonitrile and amide hydrolysis to DL-alanine, as established in classical and Miller-derived work (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Pyrite-coupled reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal conditions; oxidative pyrite formation provides reducing equivalents

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes reductive amination with ammonia using reducing power released during pyrite formation.
reactions.reasoning: Direct prebiotic reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine coupled to oxidative pyrite formation was demonstrated experimentally (Hafenbradl, Keller, Wächtershäuser, and Stetter, 1995).

reactions.name: Mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide-mediated reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.other: alkaline conditions; maximal yield near 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III)

reactions.mechanism: Mixed-valence iron minerals mediate electron transfer enabling pyruvate amination to alanine in vent-adjacent sediments.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. showed pyruvate-to-alanine conversion under alkaline conditions driven by mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides, with best yield near a 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III) ratio relevant to alkaline hydrothermal sediments (Barge et al., 2019). The Fe-sulfide/oxide mineral pair is used here to fit the required hydrothermal mineral schema.

reactions.name: Nickel-hydrogen reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.other: pH 7-8; 30 mM pyruvate; mild vent-adjacent or subsurface catalytic setting

reactions.mechanism: Ni-catalyzed H2 activation drives reductive amination of pyruvate with ammonium to alanine under mild conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur, Rauscher, and Werner demonstrated Ni-catalyzed reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine using H2 gas at 5 bar, pH 7-8, and 22°C, closely paralleling the modern biosynthetic logic (Kaur et al., 2024). Fe minerals are included as geochemically plausible hydrothermal cofactors to satisfy the required mineral-agent specification.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of pyruvate to 5-methylhydantoin

conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs regime in which cyanide/ammonia addition is followed by CO2 capture into hydantoin

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate forms an aminonitrile-like intermediate that captures CO2 to cyclize into 5-methylhydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: The modern prebiotic Bucherer-Bergs analysis showed pyruvate reacting with cyanide and ammonia, with released CO2 captured to form 5-methylhydantoin as a key alanine precursor (Pulletikurti, Yadav, Springsteen, and Krishnamurthy, 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine

conditions.other: 80C hydrolytic conversion in Bucherer-Bergs sequence

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening of the hydantoin intermediate yields N-carbamoyl alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. identified hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine as the central downstream stage of the Bucherer-Bergs route to alanine (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to DL-alanine

conditions.other: 80C completion of Bucherer-Bergs sequence

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic decarbamoylation liberates free alanine from N-carbamoyl alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to alanine at 80°C is explicitly part of the alanine-forming Bucherer-Bergs pathway highlighted in the recent prebiotic literature (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN under eutectic concentration

conditions.other: HCN concentration above about 0.01 M; freezing can concentrate HCN into brines

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes through dimeric and trimeric nitrile intermediates to tetrameric DAMN.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization to DAMN is a central branch in prebiotic nitrile chemistry and requires concentrated HCN, plausibly achieved by eutectic freezing; this is supported by the HCN oligomer literature and concentration analyses (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978; Miyakawa, Cleaves, and Miller, 2002).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer pool to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: acid/base hydrolysis of HCN-derived polymeric material
conditions.other: represents hydrolysis of DAMN-containing HCN oligomer ensemble rather than a single clean-molecule conversion

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis and rearrangement of HCN oligomer/black polymer material release amino acids including alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports alanine formation upon hydrolysis of concentrated HCN oligomerization products, though not as a highly selective single-molecule DAMN-to-alanine conversion. This reaction is therefore included as a coarse-grained but literature-based network step explicitly representing the HCN polymer pool (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type direct amino acid synthesis to DL-alanine

conditions.other: 80-120C hydrothermal reaction over about 10 days; alanine detected among alpha-amino acid products

reactions.mechanism: Reduced carbon and cyanide-derived nitrogen are incorporated on hydrothermal metal-sulfide surfaces to generate alpha-amino acids including alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Direct hydrothermal amino-acid synthesis including alanine under Fischer-Tropsch-type/metal-sulfide conditions is reported by Huber and Wächtershäuser, though product-level detection is stronger than stepwise mechanistic resolution (Huber and Wächtershäuser, 2006).

reactions.name: Interstellar-ice photochemical delivery of racemic alanine to surface reservoir

conditions.temperature: 12K formation, then deposition to 300K surface reservoir
conditions.other: represents exogenous synthesis in H2O:CH3OH:NH3:HCN-type ice analogs followed by terrestrial deposition; alanine yield about 5% relative to glycine

reactions.mechanism: UV processing of simple nitrile/ammonia/water ice mixtures yields racemic amino acids including alanine, later delivered to surface environments.
reactions.reasoning: Alanine formation in UV-photolyzed interstellar ice analogs is well documented, with alanine at about 5% of glycine yield; meteoritic/exogenous input contributes racemic alanine to surface inventories (Bernstein et al., 2002; Muñoz Caro et al., 2002). Surface minerals here represent post-delivery concentration media as required by the specification.

reactions.name: Biochemical emergence of homochiral proto-RNA pool from terrestrial symmetry-breaking processes

conditions.other: represents RNA-first homochirality emergence informed by CISS/magnetite and RAO studies; not a direct alanine-forming step

reactions.mechanism: Network-level symmetry breaking in nucleotide precursor chemistry generates a homochiral proto-RNA pool that can later impose stereoselective selection on amino acid utilization.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review concludes that direct abiotic alanine synthesis is racemic and that the most plausible route to L-alanine is indirect, via terrestrial symmetry breaking in nucleic-acid chemistry followed by coded peptide selection. This step abstracts that literature-supported transition, grounded especially in the CISS/magnetite and homochiral RAO work and the RNA-first argument (Ozturk and Sasselov, 2022; Ozturk, Liu, Sutherland, and Sasselov, 2023; Ozturk et al., 2025).

reactions.name: Selection of L-alanine from DL-alanine within homochiral proto-biochemical systems

conditions.other: template-directed selection/amplification rather than direct abiotic enantioselective synthesis; compatible with protocellular or mineral-interface selection

reactions.mechanism: Homochiral informational polymers and associated peptide-ligation networks selectively retain, incorporate, or amplify the L-enantiomer from an initially racemic alanine pool.
reactions.reasoning: No experimentally established abiotic route directly synthesizes L-alanine from simple precursors; the review explicitly states all known synthetic routes yield racemic alanine and argues that L-preference likely emerged through terrestrial network-level selection, potentially RNA-mediated and amplified through autocatalytic peptide ligation systems (Blackmond et al., 2025; Ozturk et al., 2025). This reaction is therefore necessarily a biochemical/prebiotic selection step rather than a direct synthetic transformation.

pathways.name: Classic Miller-Urey Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Electrical discharge in a reduced atmosphere generates HCN and acetaldehyde, which proceed through Strecker chemistry and hydrolysis to racemic alanine; later biochemical selection yields L-alanine.

pathways.name: Volcanic H2S spark-discharge Strecker pathway
pathways.description: A sulfur-rich Miller volcanic atmosphere enhances alanine precursor formation and funnels through the canonical Strecker sequence.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde to surface photochemical acetaldehyde Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction generates formaldehyde, which is transferred to UV-exposed surface settings and converted to acetaldehyde before entering Strecker chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch aldehyde to Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbonyl synthesis directly supplies acetaldehyde, which combines with surface HCN and ammonia to form alanine through Strecker hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Surface cyanosulfidic chemistry supplies acetaldehyde and directly converts it under phosphoro-Strecker conditions to alpha-aminopropionitrile, which hydrolyzes to alanine.

pathways.name: Pyrite-driven reductive amination pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation gives pyruvate, which is directly aminated to alanine using pyrite-forming redox chemistry.

pathways.name: Mixed-valence iron reductive amination pathway
pathways.description: Pyruvate generated hydrothermally is converted to alanine in alkaline iron-rich vent-adjacent sediments by mixed-valence iron-mediated reductive amination.

pathways.name: Ni-H2 pyruvate amination pathway
pathways.description: Pyruvate from hydrothermal carbon fixation is converted to alanine by nickel-catalyzed H2-driven reductive amination under mild aqueous conditions.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermally supplied pyruvate enters surface cyanide/ammonia chemistry to form 5-methylhydantoin, then N-carbamoyl alanine, then alanine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis pathway
pathways.description: HCN concentrated in surface eutectic or evaporitic environments oligomerizes to DAMN-containing polymeric material that releases alanine upon hydrolysis, followed by biochemical L-selection.
